Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: Analyze the Request Context PostgreSQL database with three tables claims adjusters claim_events Schema Provided Intended Process R -> A -> E -> P -> N -> C Input Python dictionary representing DECLARE model Task Identify anomalies contradictions Generate hypotheses Propose SQL queries

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Analyze the DECLARE Model init R consistent existence C consistent responded_existence E responds to A consistent precedence C activities R means if C occurs R must have occurred before consistent noncoexistence E and C cannot coexist Major Contradiction Intended process R A E P N C implies E and C both happen

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Conclusion The model is logically unsatisfiable for any trace containing A E and C If A happens E must happen If E happens C cannot happen But C must happen existence creates deadlock

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Generate Hypotheses Data Quality Extraction Error mining algorithm treated E and C as mutually exclusive Policy Change Misinterpretation of Close Technical Glitch Process Deviation fast track closing without evaluation

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - IND
Text: Propose SQL Verification Check claims with both E and C violates noncoexistence Check claims with C but no E Check claims with A but no E violates responded_existence Check claims with C but no prior R violates precedence Focus on claim_events table

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: Self-Correction on precedence semantics In DECLARE precedence A B means if A occurs B must have occurred before precedence C R means if Close happens Receive before it logically sound for intended process focus on noncoexistence conflict

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Inference existence C must happen noncoexistence E C means E cannot happen responded_existence if A then E means A cannot happen model forbids A and E entirely severe anomaly

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Refining SQL Use claim_events GROUP BY claim_id conditional aggregation SUM CASE WHEN activity E then 1 else 0 Query1 claims with both E and C Query2 claims with C no E Query3 A no E Query4 C no R PostgreSQL syntax
